name: IonMonkey MIR/LIR
summary: SpiderMonkey's IonMonkey JIT uses two IR levels: MIR (Mid-level, SSA-form, JS-aware: MAdd, MGuardShape, MCall) and LIR (Low-level, for register allocation and machine-code emission). Pointer-rich SSA, deopt snapshots at every guard.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: SpiderMonkey's IonMonkey uses two IR levels: a **MIR** (Mid-level IR, unrelated to Rust's MIR or LLVM's MIR) of SSA-form with JS-aware operations (`MAdd`, `MGuardShape`, `MCall`), and a lower **LIR** (Low-level IR) for register allocation and machine-code emission. MIR carries deopt snapshots at every guard so a failed speculation can resume into baseline code.

sections.title: Two-IR split
sections.content:
The two-IR split predates V8's Turboshaft and addresses the same separation between high-level transformation and target-specific scheduling. MIR carries operation semantics in JS terms; LIR carries operation semantics in machine terms. MIR optimisations work at the operation level; LIR handles allocation, scheduling, and emission.

The trade-off is the standard one between abstraction power (MIR) and codegen efficiency (LIR). IonMonkey accepts the translation cost as the price of clean JS-aware optimisations on the upper IR.

sections.title: Deopt snapshots at every guard
sections.content: Speculative operations carry deopt snapshots — descriptions of how to reconstruct interpreter state if the speculation fails. Tier orchestration (when the speculation falls back to lower tiers) is in `data/tiered-compilation`. Snapshots in MIR are the data substrate that the OSR/deopt mechanism consumes; their layout shapes how aggressively MIR can speculate.
